item.title: Committee says FSC profit masks deeper crisis in sugar industry
item.description: The Standing Committee on Economic Affairs says the Fiji Sugar Corporation’s reported 2025 net profit does not represent a genuine turnaround, warning that the sugar industry remains under severe structural, operational and financial pressure. Committee Chair Sakiusa Tubuna tabled the committee’s consolidated review of FSC’s 2024 and 2025 Annual Reports in Parliament on Friday.
item.content:encoded:
The Standing Committee on Economic Affairs says the Fiji Sugar Corporation’s reported 2025 net profit does not represent a genuine turnaround, warning that the sugar industry remains under severe structural, operational and financial pressure.

Committee Chair Sakiusa Tubuna tabled the committee’s consolidated review of FSC’s 2024 and 2025 Annual Reports in Parliament on Friday.

Tubuna said while FSC recorded a net profit in 2025, the committee found the result was largely driven by Government support and accounting adjustments rather than improved operational performance.

He said beneath the positive headline figure lies a worrying reality of declining sugar production, deteriorating infrastructure and continued financial vulnerability.

The committee noted that sugar production has fallen to its lowest level in more than a decade, with ongoing cane supply constraints, high levels of burnt cane and inefficiencies in harvesting and transport continuing to affect the industry.

Tubuna said FSC also remains heavily burdened by debt and dependent on continued Government assistance, raising serious concerns about its long-term financial sustainability.

The report also highlights weaknesses in governance, asset management and industry coordination, saying years of underinvestment have contributed to deteriorating infrastructure.

While FSC has announced diversification initiatives, the committee said these remain largely aspirational because they lack clear business cases, financing strategies and measurable outcomes.

The committee also expressed concern over the continued decline in the number of cane growers and the total area under cultivation, warning that this poses a direct threat to the future of the industry.

Tubuna said without urgent measures to improve the economic viability of cane farming and rebuild growers’ confidence, production targets will remain out of reach and the industry’s contraction will continue.

The report identified the high cost of production as a major challenge for farmers, particularly given that the standard 10-hectare farm size was established in the 1960s and is no longer economically sustainable.

Tubuna said around 80 percent of cane farmers produce only 20 percent of the total cane supplied to the mills, while the remaining 20 percent of growers produce 80 percent of the total tonnage. He said low cane yields and rising production costs have significantly reduced the industry’s competitiveness.

Despite the challenges, the committee acknowledged FSC’s efforts to maintain operations through mechanisation, digitalisation and diversification into new product streams, describing these initiatives as an important foundation for future reform.

However, Tubuna stressed that incremental changes would not be enough to reverse the industry’s decline.

He said the committee’s findings point to the need for decisive and coordinated reforms, including stronger governance, improved accountability, strategic investment and a fundamental reassessment of the sugar industry’s long-term business model.

The committee has recommended measures aimed at restoring operational discipline, improving transparency, reducing the industry’s reliance on Government funding and supporting a transition to a more sustainable and commercially viable future.

Tubuna thanked the FSC board, management and staff, as well as committee members, for their cooperation during the review.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Committee raises concerns over EFL’s diesel reliance, debt and transparency
item.description: The Standing Committee on Economic Affairs has raised concerns over Energy Fiji Limited’s continued reliance on imported diesel, increasing debt levels and a lack of transparency in key areas, warning these issues could undermine the long-term sustainability of Fiji’s electricity sector. Committee Chair Sakiusa Tubuna tabled the committee’s consolidated review of EFL’s 2023 and 2024 Annual Reports
item.content:encoded:
The Standing Committee on Economic Affairs has raised concerns over Energy Fiji Limited’s continued reliance on imported diesel, increasing debt levels and a lack of transparency in key areas, warning these issues could undermine the long-term sustainability of Fiji’s electricity sector.

Committee Chair Sakiusa Tubuna tabled the committee’s consolidated review of EFL’s 2023 and 2024 Annual Reports in Parliament on Friday.

Tubuna said EFL, as Fiji’s national electricity provider, plays a critical role in supporting economic growth, social development and energy security, but the committee identified several areas requiring urgent attention.

He said the committee was particularly concerned that imported diesel remains EFL’s largest operating cost, leaving the company and consumers vulnerable to fluctuations in global fuel prices.

Tubuna said despite Fiji’s target of generating 90 percent of its electricity from renewable sources by 2035, progress in reducing dependence on fossil fuels is not advancing quickly enough to meet that goal.

The committee also highlighted EFL’s increasing debt levels and ongoing financial pressures, warning that if these are not carefully managed they could affect the company’s long-term financial sustainability.

Tubuna said the review also identified shortcomings in transparency, noting there was limited disclosure on dividends paid to customer shareholders, the treatment of interest earned on customer security deposits and the outcomes of rural electrification programmes.

He said this information is essential to maintaining public confidence, promoting accountability and allowing Parliament and consumers to assess whether EFL is delivering equitable benefits to all stakeholders.

The committee also expressed concern over the significant costs incurred in hiring containerised diesel generators.

Tubuna acknowledged that rising electricity demand and climate-related impacts on hydroelectric generation present genuine challenges, but said the expenditure highlighted the need for stronger long-term planning, timely investment in renewable energy projects and more effective management of generation capacity.

To address the issues, the committee has recommended that EFL develop a clear and measurable Renewable Energy Transition Plan, strengthen its debt management strategies, improve reporting on capital expenditure and provide greater transparency on shareholder and consumer-related financial matters.

Tubuna said implementing the recommendations would improve planning, strengthen financial stability, enhance transparency and ensure investments in the energy sector deliver tangible benefits for consumers and the wider community.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Relevant ministries required to formally report back to OPM after resolving complaints
item.description: Government ministries are required to formally report back to the Office of the Prime Minister (OPM) once complaints referred to them have been resolved. This has been revealed in the Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence report tabled in Parliament on Friday following its review of the OPM’s operations.
item.content:encoded:
Government ministries are required to formally report back to the Office of the Prime Minister (OPM) once complaints referred to them have been resolved.

This has been revealed in the Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence report tabled in Parliament on Friday following its review of the OPM’s operations.

The report says the OPM serves as a central point for receiving public complaints lodged through walk-ins, emails, letters or raised directly with the Prime Minister during public engagements. Rather than resolving the matters itself, the OPM refers each complaint to the relevant line ministry for investigation and action.

According to the OPM, ministries must follow a structured two-step reporting process.

After investigating the complaint and taking corrective action, the responsible ministry is required to prepare a formal closure file containing a summary of its findings, details of the actions taken to resolve the issue and evidence of the resolution, such as signed inspection forms, receipts or official correspondence sent to the complainant.

The report says the ministry must then submit a formal Return Memorandum to the OPM to officially close the case and maintain administrative accountability.

The accountability process is managed through Permanent Secretaries, with the Permanent Secretary of the relevant ministry required to send the Return Memorandum or resolution report directly to the Permanent Secretary of the Office of the Prime Minister.

The final report must clearly explain how the complaint was resolved and confirm that the case has been formally closed by the ministry.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Narsey urges CRC to adopt earlier electoral reform blueprint
item.description: Economist Professor Wadan Narsey has called on the Constitution Review Commission (CRC) to adopt the recommendations of the 2025 Fiji Electoral Law Reform Commission (FELRC), arguing that the work has already addressed many of the electoral issues now under review. In a submission to the CRC, Narsey said the 2025 FELRC report represented “a significant milestone” in Fiji’s democratic development b
item.content:encoded:
Economist Professor Wadan Narsey has called on the Constitution Review Commission (CRC) to adopt the recommendations of the 2025 Fiji Electoral Law Reform Commission (FELRC), arguing that the work has already addressed many of the electoral issues now under review.

In a submission to the CRC, Narsey said the 2025 FELRC report represented “a significant milestone” in Fiji’s democratic development but was never tabled in Parliament or released publicly despite being completed and presented to Government.

Narsey, who served on the FELRC chaired by former Chief Justice Daniel Fatiaki, said the Commission was tasked with strengthening democracy, improving accountability, promoting women’s representation and consulting widely with the public on electoral reform.

He said public consultations identified widespread dissatisfaction with the current electoral system, including the complex ballot paper, the lack of identifiable local Members of Parliament, and concerns that candidates with relatively few votes could enter Parliament while others with larger personal vote totals missed out.

To address those concerns, the FELRC proposed a Proportional Open List Constituency System (POLCS) comprising 25 open constituencies, 25 reserved seats for women and 21 proportional list seats, creating a 71-member Parliament.

According to Narsey, the model would preserve proportional representation while restoring local constituency representation and making MPs more directly accountable to voters.

He said the proposal would simplify voting by replacing the current numbered ballot with candidate names, party symbols and photographs, while vote counting would occur openly at constituency centres before party-list seats were allocated nationally.

Narsey also defended the recommendation for reserved seats for women, arguing that Fiji’s low level of female representation in Parliament justifies temporary special measures.

He said the proposal would ensure at least 25 women MPs, helping Fiji meet international commitments under the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women (CEDAW) while giving women voters greater parliamentary representation.

The submission also recommends reducing the party threshold for proportional representation from five per cent to two per cent, saying the current threshold effectively discards thousands of votes cast for smaller parties.

Narsey argues that the recommended electoral model would reduce opportunities for gerrymandering, improve transparency, allow independent candidates and smaller parties a greater chance of election, and strengthen democratic accountability.

However, he expressed doubt the reforms would be implemented before the next general election, suggesting the current electoral system remains politically advantageous to Fiji’s existing leaders despite public support for change.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: FRCS key management pay rises to $1.58 million
item.description: Remuneration for the Fiji Revenue and Customs Service’s (FRCS) key management personnel increased by more than $230,000 during the 2024-2025 financial year, according to the agency’s annual report tabled in Parliament on Friday. The report shows that salary and other benefits paid to key management personnel rose from $1,349,398 in the year ended 31 July 2024 to $1,581,062 for the year ended 31 Ju
item.content:encoded:
Remuneration for the Fiji Revenue and Customs Service’s (FRCS) key management personnel increased by more than $230,000 during the 2024-2025 financial year, according to the agency’s annual report tabled in Parliament on Friday.

The report shows that salary and other benefits paid to key management personnel rose from $1,349,398 in the year ended 31 July 2024 to $1,581,062 for the year ended 31 July 2025—an increase of $231,664, or about 17 per cent.

Directors’ remuneration also increased significantly, rising from $71,499 in 2023-2024 to $146,301 in 2024-2025.

The annual report defines key management personnel as those with the authority and responsibility for planning, directing and controlling the activities of the organisation, whether directly or indirectly.

During the reporting period, the FRCS key management team comprised Chief Executive Officer Udit Singh, Director Corporate Services and Chief of Staff Shavindra Nath, Director Compliance Kelerayani Dawai, Director People, Capability and Culture Uraia Rasake, Director Border Forces Shelini Devi, Director Tax Momina Beg, Chief Financial Officer Ansu Singh, Chief Information Officer Nalin Ranawalage, and Deputy Director Governance and Risk Ilaisa Lakepa.

The report notes that directors’ remuneration includes amounts paid to FRCS directors, with remuneration for Chairman Shiri Gounder paid directly by the Ministry of Finance. It also states that remuneration for Malakai Naiyaga, Neelesh Gounder, Nitin Gandhi, Jitoko Tikolevu and Umesh Prasad was paid directly to those directors.

The remuneration figures are contained in the 2024-2025 FRCS Annual Report, which was tabled in Parliament on Friday.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: FRCS waives $184.6 million in tax penalties
item.description: The Fiji Revenue and Customs Service (FRCS) waived or wrote off $184.6 million in tax penalties during the 2024-2025 financial year, according to its annual report tabled in Parliament on Friday. The report states that $184,556,648 in penalties was waived or written off for the year ended 31 July 2025, an increase of more than $75 million compared to the $109,513,509 waived during the previous fin
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Revenue and Customs Service (FRCS) waived or wrote off $184.6 million in tax penalties during the 2024-2025 financial year, according to its annual report tabled in Parliament on Friday.

The report states that $184,556,648 in penalties was waived or written off for the year ended 31 July 2025, an increase of more than $75 million compared to the $109,513,509 waived during the previous financial year.

According to FRCS, the withdrawals were made in accordance with Section 48(7) of the Tax Administration Act and Section 34 of the Financial Management Act 2004.

The annual report also reveals that total government debtor arrears stood at $495.39 million as at 31 July 2025.

The largest share comprised Income Tax and Other Taxes, amounting to $280.29 million, followed by Value Added Tax (VAT) arrears of $181.95 million. Other outstanding debts included MSME loans of $30.79 million and Customs arrears of $2.35 million.

FRCS noted that the arrears figures include penalties that may be waived as well as balances that are subject to dispute by taxpayers.

The report further states that during the financial year, responsibility for the Tertiary Education Loan Scheme (TELS) and Public Service Commission (PSC) loan portfolios was transferred to the Tertiary Scholarships and Loans Service (TSLS), resulting in those balances no longer being reflected under FRCS debtors.

The figures are contained in the 2024-2025 FRCS Annual Report, which was tabled in Parliament on Friday.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Police investigate social media threat
item.description: The Fiji Police Force’s Criminal Investigations Department is investigating a social media post threatening unrest in Suva and calling for the removal of Fiji’s Constitution. In a statement, Police said the post issued an ultimatum demanding the Constitution be removed and threatened to burn the nation’s capital.
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Police Force’s Criminal Investigations Department is investigating a social media post threatening unrest in Suva and calling for the removal of Fiji’s Constitution.

In a statement, Police said the post issued an ultimatum demanding the Constitution be removed and threatened to burn the nation’s capital.

The matter has been referred to the Criminal Investigations Department, with Digital Forensic investigators now examining the post to identify those responsible and determine whether any criminal offences have been committed.

Police have reminded members of the public to use social media responsibly, warning that while freedom of expression and speech is protected, it must be exercised within the boundaries of the law.

The Force stressed that the abuse of social media platforms and any actions that breach the law will not be tolerated.

Investigations into the origin of the post are continuing.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Drug raids under scrutiny
item.description: ALLEGATIONS of brutality during drug raids, including incidents involving deaths, have prompted the Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission to closely monitor joint operations. Consequently, the commission is tracking the involvement of the Republic of Fiji Military Forces alongside the Fiji Police Force.
item.content:encoded:
ALLEGATIONS of brutality during drug raids, including incidents involving deaths, have prompted the Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission to closely monitor joint operations.

Consequently, the commission is tracking the involvement of the Republic of Fiji Military Forces alongside the Fiji Police Force.

Commissioner Chantelle Khan said they were monitoring operations due to recent alleged brutality complaints and increased military involvement in drug operations.

“That is a very sensitive issue. Many members of the public support military involvement because they believe it improves public safety,” she said.

Ms Khan said the issue was sensitive, particularly as many members of the public supported military involvement because they believed it would improve public safety.

“Our role is to ensure human rights are protected during operations involving civilians. We are monitoring their role.”

She said they had arranged meetings with the military and police to better understand the framework guiding joint operations, including the roles and responsibilities of officers involved.

“We want to better understand the terms of reference governing joint military-police operations, where responsibilities begin and end.

“Also, whether officers involved fully understand their human rights obligations when carrying out operations involving civilians.”

She said Fiji was not under a state of emergency, due to this, human rights complaints would be handled through standard commission filings or official investigations.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
